description: The figure displays ball-and-stick crystal structure models of Fe-Mn-Al alloys investigated through first-principles density functional theory calculations. The left subfigure presents a cubic supercell of the B2-ordered structure (space group Pm-3m), with purple spheres representing Fe atoms at cube corners and blue spheres representing Al atoms at body-center positions. The right subfigure illustrates the same structural framework but highlights Mn substitution, with a distinct green sphere indicating a Mn atom replacing an Fe site in the Fe1-xMnxAl alloy system. These models visualize the atomic arrangements used as input for calculating formation enthalpies, cohesive energies, electronic density of states, and elastic mechanical properties across the composition series from FeAl to MnAl. The structures maintain cubic symmetry with varying Mn content, serving as the geometric basis for investigating thermodynamic stability, bulk/shear moduli, and Debye temperatures reported in the computational study.

The image displays a crystal structure model of Fe-Mn-Al alloys, showing a cubic supercell with B2 ordered structure (space group Pm-3m). The ball-and-stick representation illustrates different atomic species occupying specific lattice sites: purple spheres represent Fe atoms, gray spheres represent Al atoms, and green spheres represent Mn atoms in the substitutional solid solution. This structural model represents the computational supercell used in first-principles DFT calculations (CASTEP code with GGA-PBE, 450 eV cutoff, 8×8×8 k-points) to investigate nine compositions ranging from FeAl to MnAl (Fe1-xMnxAl, x = 0 to 1). The visualization demonstrates the atomic arrangement and lattice symmetry that underpins the calculated electronic structures, formation enthalpies, elastic constants (C11, C12, C44), and mechanical properties (bulk modulus, shear modulus, Young's modulus) reported in the study. The model specifically supports the investigation of how Mn substitution affects the thermodynamic stability, bonding character (covalent/metallic mix), and elastic anisotropy of the B2-phase Fe-Mn-Al system.

The image displays a three-dimensional ball-and-stick model of a cubic crystal structure representing the Fe–Mn–Al alloy system investigated via first-principles calculations. The supercell visualization features three distinct atomic species represented by colored spheres—pink/purple, gray, and green—corresponding to Fe, Mn, and Al atoms respectively. This structural model illustrates the B2-ordered cubic phase (space group Pm-3m) with atoms occupying specific lattice sites including corner and body-centered positions. The figure visualizes the crystallographic framework used to calculate formation enthalpy, cohesive energy, electronic density of states, and elastic constants across the compositional series from FeAl to MnAl. It demonstrates the atomic coordination environment and substitution patterns of Mn for Fe in the intermetallic lattice, providing the structural basis for understanding the mechanical anisotropy, bonding characteristics (covalent and metallic), and thermodynamic stability reported in the density functional theory study.

The figure displays a ball-and-stick crystal structure model of the Fe-Mn-Al alloy system, specifically illustrating a B2 (CsCl-type) ordered cubic supercell (space group Pm-3m) used in first-principles DFT calculations. The model uses color-coded spheres to distinguish different atomic species: purple spheres represent Fe atoms occupying one sublattice, while green and grey spheres represent Mn and Al atoms (respectively) positioned at the body-center sites or substituted within the Fe sublattice. The blue lines delineate the cubic unit cell boundaries, showing the periodic arrangement and coordination environment of atoms. This structural visualization corresponds to the computational models employed to investigate the composition-dependent properties across the Fe1-xMnxAl series (from FeAl to MnAl), serving as the structural basis for calculating formation enthalpies, electronic density of states, elastic constants, and Debye temperatures reported in the study.

The image displays a three-dimensional ball-and-stick crystal structure model of a Fe–Mn–Al alloy, illustrating the B2 ordered cubic structure (space group Pm-3m) employed in the first-principles DFT calculations. The model shows a cubic supercell with purple spheres representing Fe atoms, green spheres representing Mn atoms, and gray spheres representing Al atoms, arranged in a CsCl-type configuration where Fe/Mn atoms occupy body-center positions and Al atoms occupy corner positions. The visualization demonstrates the atomic arrangement and substitutional site occupancy in the Fe1-xMnxAl system, showing Mn substitution for Fe within the B2 lattice framework outlined by blue lines. This structural model represents the computational basis for investigating formation enthalpy, cohesive energy, electronic density of states, and mechanical properties including elastic constants, bulk modulus, shear modulus, and Young's modulus reported in the study.

The figure displays a ball-and-stick crystal structure model of Fe-Mn-Al alloys with B2 ordered structure (space group Pm-3m), representing the cubic supercell used in first-principles DFT calculations. The model visualizes a 2×2×2 supercell or unit cell with atoms depicted as spheres: purple spheres represent Fe atoms (or Fe/Mn shared lattice sites), green spheres represent Al atoms, and the distinct blue/gray sphere indicates Mn substitution at specific Fe sites, illustrating the solid solution formation across the Fe1-xMnxAl composition series. This structural representation supports the computational investigation of chemical stability, electronic density of states, and mechanical properties including elastic constants, bulk modulus, and Debye temperature variations with Mn content. The B2 structure features a CsCl-type arrangement where Al occupies one simple cubic sublattice while Fe and Mn statistically occupy the other sublattice. The visualization provides the geometric foundation for understanding atomic coordination, bond character (mixed covalent-metallic), and structural anisotropy that underpins the reported mechanical behavior and thermodynamic stability trends from FeAl to MnAl.

The image displays a ball-and-stick model of the Fe–Mn–Al alloy crystal structure, specifically illustrating the B2 ordered cubic supercell (space group Pm-3m) used in the first-principles DFT calculations. The violet spheres represent Fe atoms occupying the corner positions of the cubic unit cell, while the green and blue spheres represent Al and Mn atoms respectively at the body-center positions, demonstrating the substitutional solid solution of Mn in the FeAl lattice. This structural visualization corresponds to the Fe₁₋ₓMnₓAl alloy system investigated in the study, showing the atomic arrangement for compositions ranging from FeAl to MnAl. The figure visually supports the computational setup described in the paper, where Mn content varies from 0 to 100% substitution of Fe sites, maintaining the CsCl-type B2 structure throughout the composition range. This crystal structure model serves as the basis for calculating formation enthalpy, cohesive energy, electronic density of states, and elastic constants reported in the study.

The figure displays a three-dimensional ball-and-stick representation of the Fe_{1-x}Mn_xAl alloy crystal structure alongside atomic legend insets. The primary visualization shows a cubic supercell (space group Pm-3m) characteristic of the B2 ordered structure, where purple spheres represent atoms on the Fe/Mn sublattice and green spheres represent Al atoms. The three smaller images serve as elemental legends, indicating the distinct atomic species used in the structural model. According to the caption and citation context, this structure represents a solid solution series where Mn atoms progressively substitute for Fe atoms in the FeAl lattice (x = 0.125n, n = 0–8). The model illustrates the atomic arrangement used for first-principles DFT calculations, establishing the crystallographic basis for investigating formation enthalpy, cohesive energy, elastic properties, and electronic structure across the compositional range from FeAl to MnAl.

This figure presents a dual-axis line plot showing the compositional dependence of thermodynamic stability parameters in the Fe₁₋ₓMnₓAl alloy system calculated via density functional theory. The x-axis represents Mn content ranging from 0 to 1.0 (corresponding to FeAl to MnAl), while the left y-axis displays cohesive energy (E_coh) in eV·atom⁻¹ and the right y-axis shows formation enthalpy (ΔH_f) in eV·atom⁻¹. Red squares denote the cohesive energy curve, which increases from approximately -6.95 to -6.58 eV·atom⁻¹ as Mn content rises, while blue squares represent the formation enthalpy curve, increasing from about -0.38 to -0.22 eV·atom⁻¹. Both curves exhibit a monotonic upward trend (becoming less negative) with increasing Mn substitution, indicating decreasing thermodynamic stability. FeAl (x=0) possesses the lowest formation enthalpy (-0.391 eV·atom⁻¹) and cohesive energy (-6.947 eV·atom⁻¹), establishing it as the most stable phase in the series. The negative values across all compositions confirm that the entire Fe₁₋ₓMnₓAl system remains thermodynamically stable, supporting the suitability of these alloys for high-temperature structural applications.

This figure presents first-principles DFT calculations of the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) for the Fe1-xMnxAl alloy system across the complete compositional range from FeAl to MnAl. The ten panels (a-j) display spin-polarized DOS curves for: (a) FeAl, (b) Fe0.875Mn0.125Al, (c) Fe0.75Mn0.25Al, (d) Fe0.625Mn0.375Al, (e) Fe0.5Mn0.5Al, (f) Fe0.375Mn0.625Al, (g) pure Fe, (h) Fe0.25Mn0.75Al, (i) Fe0.125Mn0.875Al, and (j) MnAl. Each subplot shows the up-states (positive y-axis, black curves) and down-states (negative y-axis, red curves) with contributions from Al-s (dash-dot), Al-p (dotted), Fe-d (dashed), and Mn-d (dash-dot-dot) orbitals. The vertical dashed lines mark the Fermi level (0 eV). The non-zero DOS at the Fermi level across all compositions confirms metallic behavior, while the asymmetry between spin-up and spin-down states indicates magnetic characteristics. The citation notes that the sharp peaks near the Fermi level primarily originate from Fe-d and Mn-d states hybridized with Al-p states in the -5 to 1 eV range, supporting the conclusion of combined covalent and metallic bonding in these B2-structured alloys.

This figure presents first-principles DFT calculation results showing the total electron density distribution (left column: a, c, e, g, i) and electron density difference distribution (right column: b, d, f, h, j) for the Fe₁₋ₓMnₓAl alloy system across varying manganese concentrations. The subfigures display 2D contour maps for FeAl-(011) (a, b), Fe₀.₇₅Mn₀.₂₅Al-(011) (c, d), Fe₀.₅Mn₀.₅Al-(110) (e, f), Fe₀.₂₅Mn₀.₇₅Al-(110) (g, h), and MnAl-(110) (i, j), with color scales indicating electron density magnitudes ranging from 0.075 to 0.750 for total density and -0.060 to 0.080 for difference maps. Purple spheres represent Al atoms, blue spheres represent Fe atoms, and green spheres represent Mn atoms. The total electron density maps reveal elongated contours along Al-Fe, Al-Mn, and Fe-Mn bond axes indicating covalent interactions, while the difference maps show electron delocalization in interstitial regions (red/yellow) and electron localization around atomic cores (blue). These visualizations demonstrate the coexistence of covalent and metallic bonding character across the compositional series, supporting the conclusion that these bonding features contribute to high melting points and hardness in Fe-Mn-Al alloys.

This figure presents the composition-dependent elastic properties of Fe1-xMnxAl alloys calculated via first-principles DFT methods. The graph displays two distinct curves plotted against Mn content (x) ranging from 0 to 1.0: Young's modulus (E, red squares, left y-axis in GPa) and the elastic constant difference (C11-C12, blue squares, right y-axis). Both parameters exhibit a similar V-shaped variation trend, decreasing sharply from FeAl (x=0, E≈296 GPa, C11-C12≈160 GPa) to reach minimum values at Fe0.5Mn0.5Al (x=0.5, E≈202 GPa, C11-C12≈73 GPa), then increasing to local maxima near Fe0.125Mn0.875Al (x=0.875) before dropping again at MnAl (x=1.0). The synchronous variation of Young's modulus and C11-C12 indicates that the tetragonal shear modulus and stiffness decrease significantly with Mn substitution up to equiatomic Fe-Mn composition, suggesting Fe0.5Mn0.5Al possesses the highest compressibility and lowest stiffness in this alloy series.

Figure 6 presents the compositional dependence of elastic moduli in the Fe1-xMnxAl alloy system calculated via first-principles DFT. The graph plots bulk modulus (B, black squares), shear modulus (G, red circles), and elastic constant C44 (blue triangles) as functions of Mn content (x = 0 to 1.0). The bulk modulus decreases monotonically from approximately 187 GPa for FeAl to 170 GPa for MnAl. In contrast, the shear modulus and C44 exhibit non-monotonic behavior, reaching minimum values at the equiatomic composition Fe0.5Mn0.5Al (G ≈ 77 GPa, C44 ≈ 126 GPa), with C44 showing a subsequent peak near x = 0.8. These trends indicate that Fe0.5Mn0.5Al possesses the highest ductility and lowest hardness among the series, as supported by the accompanying text noting this composition exhibits maximum C12, minimum C11-C12, and maximum Poisson's ratio. The data demonstrate distinct mechanical property evolution pathways for B versus G and C44 upon Mn substitution.

This figure presents a dual-axis line plot illustrating the compositional dependence of Poisson's ratio (δ) and the B/G ratio (bulk-to-shear modulus ratio, inverse of Pugh's modulus ratio) in the Fe_{1-x}Mn_xAl alloy system calculated via first-principles DFT. The x-axis represents Mn content (x) ranging from 0 (FeAl) to 1.0 (MnAl), while the left y-axis displays Poisson's ratio (red squares, δ) and the right y-axis shows B/G values (blue squares). Both curves exhibit nearly identical variation trends: they increase gradually from FeAl (Poisson's ratio ≈0.236, B/G≈1.56), reach a maximum at Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5}Al (Poisson's ratio ≈0.309, B/G≈2.28), then decrease to a local minimum around Fe_{0.25}Mn_{0.75}Al before rising sharply toward MnAl (Poisson's ratio ≈0.324, B/G≈2.52). According to the Pugh criterion where B/G>1.75 indicates ductility, the figure reveals that FeAl and Fe_{0.875}Mn_{0.125}Al are brittle, while alloys with higher Mn content become ductile, with Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5}Al possessing the optimal balance of hardness and ductility among the studied compositions.

This figure presents a dual-axis line plot showing the compositional dependence of elastic anisotropy indices in the Fe$_{1-x}$Mn$_x$Al alloy system calculated via first-principles DFT. The x-axis represents Mn content ranging from 0 to 1.0, covering alloys from FeAl to MnAl. The left y-axis (red circles, ranging 0.5–4.0) displays the universal anisotropic index $A^U$, while the right y-axis (blue squares, ranging 0.05–0.30) shows the shear anisotropic index $A_G$. Both indices exhibit similar non-monotonic trends: they increase with Mn content initially, reaching a local maximum around $x=0.5$ (Fe$_{0.5}$Mn$_{0.5}$Al), then decrease slightly between $x=0.5$ and $x=0.75$, before rising sharply at $x=1.0$ (MnAl). The plot reveals that MnAl possesses the highest elastic anisotropy ($A^U\approx3.8$) among the studied compositions, whereas FeAl ($x=0$) shows the lowest anisotropy ($A^U\approx0.56$). This figure supports the computational conclusion that Mn substitution significantly modulates the elastic anisotropy of Fe-Al based alloys, with the mixed intermediate compositions displaying variable anisotropic behavior between the two end members.

Figure 9 presents planar projections of Young's modulus for the Fe₁₋ₓMnₓAl alloy system (x ranging from 0 to 1) on (001) and (110) crystallographic planes, illustrating the directional dependence and elastic anisotropy calculated from first-principles DFT. The left subplot displays the (001) plane projection with axes oriented along [100] and [010] directions, while the right subplot shows the (110) plane projection along [1̄10] and [001] directions, with Young's modulus values plotted in GPa. Nine distinct compositions are represented by different line styles: FeAl, Fe₀.₈₇₅Mn₀.₁₂₅Al, Fe₀.₇₅Mn₀.₂₅Al, Fe₀.₆₂₅Mn₀.₃₇₅Al, Fe₀.₅Mn₀.₅Al, Fe₀.₃₇₅Mn₀.₆₂₅Al, Fe₀.₂₅Mn₀.₇₅Al, Fe₀.₁₂₅Mn₀.₈₇₅Al, and MnAl. The distinctly non-spherical, butterfly-shaped contour patterns demonstrate strong elastic anisotropy across all compositions. As reported in the caption and citation text, Fe₀.₁₂₅Mn₀.₈₇₅Al exhibits the maximum Young's modulus along the principal crystallographic directions, while MnAl shows the minimum values. These polar plots visually confirm that the planar contours deviate significantly from circular shapes, supporting the conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al alloys possess substantial directional variation in stiffness essential for high-temperature structural applications.

A dual-axis line graph presenting the calculated Vickers hardness ($H_V$, red squares, left y-axis in GPa) and Debye temperature ($\Theta_D$, blue squares, right y-axis in K) as functions of Mn content ($x$) in the Fe$_{1-x}$Mn$_x$Al alloy system, spanning from FeAl ($x$=0) to MnAl ($x$=1.0). Both properties exhibit a similar non-monotonic trend: they decrease sharply from FeAl (approximately 16.5 GPa and 652.5 K) to a minimum at Fe$_{0.5}$Mn$_{0.5}$Al ($x$=0.5, approximately 6.7 GPa and 533.3 K), subsequently increase to a local maximum near Fe$_{0.25}$Mn$_{0.75}$Al ($x$=0.75, approximately 9.8 GPa and 574.2 K), and finally decrease toward MnAl (approximately 5.0 GPa and 504.1 K). This correlation suggests that Mn addition weakens covalent bonding character up to the equiatomic composition, with the synchronous variation of hardness and Debye temperature reflecting the underlying changes in chemical bonding and elastic properties across the alloy series.
